sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Nebulisation delivers aerosolised medication to the airways, most commonly bronchodilators. In prehospital care it is used when inhaled therapy is indicated and the patient cannot effectively use an MDI with spacer, or when local practice prefers nebulisation for the severity of presentation.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Assemble the chamber, tubing, and mask or mouthpiece
2. Add the prescribed medication and diluent if required by protocol or product instructions
3. Drive the nebuliser with the appropriate gas source and flow for the clinical situation
4. Sit the patient upright if possible and coach normal tidal breathing unless another technique is specifically required
5. Reassess work of breathing, auscultation, respiratory rate, and oxygenation during and after treatment

sections.title: Clinical Considerations
sections.content:
- Oxygen-driven nebulisation may not be appropriate for every patient, especially if a lower oxygen target or air-driven delivery is needed
- Nebulisation can increase aerosol spread, so infection-control considerations matter
- Severe fatigue, worsening wheeze, silent chest, or falling conscious state require escalation beyond repeated bronchodilator delivery
- Response to treatment should guide whether therapy is repeated, changed, or escalated

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Document the medication, dose, driving gas, flow, and patient response
- Keep the patient under observation during treatment, especially if very unwell
- Nebulisation is one part of respiratory management and should not delay escalation when the patient is tiring or deteriorating
